description: The sand tiger shark's rows of protruding, ragged teeth give it a ferocious appearance, yet it is one of the most docile sharks toward humans. It is the only shark known to gulp air at the surface and hold it in its stomach to achieve near-neutral buoyancy — allowing it to hover motionless in the water column. Found in warm and temperate coastal waters worldwide, it is a slow-reproducing species with a highly unusual embryonic development: pups cannibalize each other in the womb, and only two survive to birth (one per uterus).
funFact: Sand tiger shark embryos engage in intrauterine cannibalism — the first embryo to develop a functional jaw actively hunts and consumes its siblings in the womb. Only one pup per uterus survives, emerging as an already-experienced predator about 1 m long at birth.
habitat: Warm temperate to tropical coastal waters worldwide, typically found near the seafloor in surf zones, shallow bays, rocky reefs, and around offshore islands. Depth range extends from the intertidal zone to at least 191 m. Also commonly found in aquariums due to their docility.
diet: Feeds mainly on bony fish (eels, snappers, flatfish, bluefish), squid, and crustaceans. Uses near-neutral buoyancy to hover motionless before ambushing prey. Will consume small sharks and rays when opportunity arises.
size: Typically 2.0–2.5 m (6.6–8.2 ft); maximum recorded 3.2 m (10.5 ft). Females grow slightly larger than males.

vitalSigns.label: Estimated Population
vitalSigns.value: Severely depleted in most regions — no reliable global estimate; Eastern Australia estimated ~1,500–2,000 adults

vitalSigns.label: Reproductive Rate
vitalSigns.value: 1 pup per uterus (2 per litter); ~9–12 month gestation — lowest reproductive rate of any shark

vitalSigns.label: Diet
vitalSigns.value: Bony fish, squid, rays, small sharks, crustaceans

vitalSigns.label: Population Growth Rate
vitalSigns.value: Extremely slow — produces only 2 pups every 2 years; recovery from depletion may take decades

vitalSigns.label: Key Adaptations
vitalSigns.value: Gulps air at the surface to fill stomach and achieve neutral buoyancy; the only shark species known to use this mechanism for hovering motionless in the water column

habitatStats.label: Global Range
habitatStats.value: Warm temperate and tropical coastal waters worldwide; notably absent from the eastern Pacific Ocean

habitatStats.label: Depth Range
habitatStats.value: Intertidal zone to 191 m (627 ft); most common at 0–25 m on rocky reefs and sandy flats

threats.name: Historical Directed Fishing
threats.description: Hunted extensively across its range through the 1980s for food, liver oil, and sport. Eastern Australian populations were decimated — estimated at fewer than 1,500 adults before receiving protection in 1984.

threats.name: Beach Meshing Programs
threats.description: Shark nets deployed along Australian and South African coastlines to protect swimmers kill significant numbers annually. In New South Wales alone, hundreds have been caught over the past three decades.

threats.name: Bycatch
threats.description: Caught in gillnets, longlines, and fish traps throughout their range. Low reproductive rate means even modest ongoing mortality prevents population recovery.

threats.name: Habitat Degradation
threats.description: Estuarine and near-shore nursery habitats are affected by coastal development, runoff, and pollution, reducing the sheltered areas where juveniles grow.

actionItems.title: Oppose Shark Culling Programs
actionItems.description: Beach meshing programs along Australian and South African coastlines kill sand tigers regularly. Contact local representatives and support shark-safe alternatives like drumlines with escape mechanisms and personal deterrents.

actionItems.title: Support Marine Protected Areas
actionItems.description: Advocate for well-enforced MPAs around key sand tiger aggregation sites, including the reefs of eastern Australia and the US Atlantic coast where populations are slowly recovering.

actionItems.title: Report Strandings and Sightings
actionItems.description: In Australia and South Africa, reporting sand tiger strandings and sightings to wildlife authorities provides critical data for population monitoring programs.

actionItems.title: Donate to Shark Trust
actionItems.description: The Shark Trust funds research and advocacy for vulnerable shark species worldwide, including ongoing work on sand tiger population recovery.

healthMetrics.label: IUCN Status
healthMetrics.value: Vulnerable — listed since 1996; Eastern Australian population classified Critically Endangered separately

healthMetrics.label: Population Trend
healthMetrics.value: Decreasing globally — Eastern Australia down >95% before protections; US Atlantic recovering slowly from depletion

healthMetrics.label: Habitat Quality
healthMetrics.value: Moderate — rocky reefs and shoals largely intact in key regions; coastal nursery habitats degraded in some areas

healthMetrics.label: Prey Availability
healthMetrics.value: Moderate — schooling fish and reef prey remain available across most of range; some localized depletion

healthMetrics.label: Fishing Pressure
healthMetrics.value: High — beach nets ongoing in Australia and South Africa; bycatch in gillnets and longlines; improving in some regions

healthMetrics.label: Protection Coverage
healthMetrics.value: Partial — protected in US, Australia, South Africa, and Mediterranean; unprotected or poorly managed in remaining range

populationTrendMeta.note: No reliable global population estimate exists. Eastern Australia's population was estimated at fewer than 1,500 adults by the early 1980s, driving full protection. Global figures are loosely modeled from documented regional declines and slow recovery rates. Actual totals are unknown.

preyDeclineRegions.name: Eastern Australia
preyDeclineRegions.note: Historical prey depletion in heavily fished coastal zones; some recovery under fisheries management

preyDeclineRegions.name: US Atlantic Coast
preyDeclineRegions.note: Schooling fish moderately depleted in historical nursery areas; partial recovery ongoing

preyDeclineRegions.name: Mediterranean
preyDeclineRegions.note: Coastal fish and cephalopods heavily depleted by intensive inshore fisheries

preyDeclineRegions.name: S. Africa
preyDeclineRegions.note: Localized prey depletion near major population centers; some managed fisheries in place

fishingPressureRegions.name: Eastern Australia
fishingPressureRegions.note: Beach meshing programs kill sand tigers annually; historically decimated by directed hunting; now protected but recovery is slow

fishingPressureRegions.name: US Atlantic Coast
fishingPressureRegions.note: Bycatch in gillnets and bottom trawls; beach nets at some locations; improving since 1997 federal protection

fishingPressureRegions.name: Mediterranean
fishingPressureRegions.note: Historically targeted; ongoing bycatch in longlines and trawls; minimal dedicated protections

fishingPressureRegions.name: S. Africa
fishingPressureRegions.note: Protected species but shark nets along KwaZulu-Natal coast continue to catch individuals; monitoring in place
